text: Hybrid porous nanowire arrays of strongly coupled Co3O4 and carbon were fabricated by carbonizing the metal-organic framework (MOF) precursors on Cu foil in a simple thermal process. The obtained sample exhibited large surface area of 251 m(2) g(-1) and high carbon content of 52.1 wt %. Interestingly, it can work as a standalone oxygen evolution electrode material without any current collectors or binders. Consequently, it can successfully catalyze the oxygen evolution reaction (OER) in both 0.1 and 1.0 M KOH solutions with a low onset potential of 1.47 V versus the Reversible Hydrogen Electrode (RHE) and robust stability of 10.0 mA cm(-2) at 1.52 V (vs. RHE) for over 30 h in 0.1 M KOH solution with a high Faradaic efficiency of 99.3%. The ultrahigh OER activity and stability (comparable to or exceeding that of most of the reported noble-metal/transition-metal and nonmetal electrocatalysts) in the Co3O4-based nanomaterials is for the first time achieved by using nanowire array electrode material and in situ grown carbon in the hybrid material, ascribed to the large electrode area, enhanced kinetics of mass and charge transportation, easier gas bubble release and stable structure. Importantly, the hybrid Co3O4-carbon porous nanowire arrays materials were also found to catalyze oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) with the four-electron reduction pathway for the reversible oxygen evolution and reduction processes, which is beneficial for rechargeable metal-air batteries and regenerative fuel cells as well as other sustainable energy storage and conversion devices.
